title: Wives, Husbands, and Suffering for Righteousness
subtitle: Submission in marriage, blessing instead of reviling, and readiness to give an answer for hope

heb.paragraph: Wives, be subject to your own husbands - the same verb used for submission to government (2:13) and masters (2:18). The pattern is ordered deference, not servile cowering.
Respectful and pure conduct - the fear is reverent respect, not terror. The goal is to win unbelieving husbands without a word.
Heirs together of the grace of life - husbands and wives are co-heirs. Spiritual equality undergirds the household order.

panels.ctx: Peter continues the household code. Wives married to unbelievers are to win them without a word through respectful conduct. Inner beauty (the hidden person of the heart) matters more than external adornment. Sarah is the model - she obeyed Abraham and called him lord. Husbands must live with wives in an understanding way, showing honor to the weaker vessel as fellow heirs of grace - so that their prayers will not be hindered.

cross.note: Wives, submit to your own husbands, as to the Lord.
Sarah laughed and said, After I am worn out, and my lord is old, shall I have pleasure?
If any woman has an unbelieving husband who consents to live with her, she should not divorce him.
Husbands, love your wives, and do not be harsh with them.

notes.note: Wives, be subject to your husbands, so that even if some do not obey the word, they may be won without a word by the conduct of their wives.
Do not let your adorning be external - braiding hair, gold jewelry, fine clothing - but the hidden person of the heart with imperishable beauty.
Holy women who hoped in God used to adorn themselves by submitting to their husbands, as Sarah obeyed Abraham and called him lord.
Husbands, live with your wives in an understanding way, showing honor as to the weaker vessel and as heirs together of grace, so that your prayers may not be hindered.
Calvin notes that the goal is evangelism through conduct. Words can provoke; consistent godliness convinces. Sarah exemplifies willing submission.
The warning about hindered prayers is serious. Marital discord disrupts the spiritual life. Husbands must honor wives as equal heirs.
The phrase without a word means silent witness through conduct, not verbal preaching. The Greek logos can mean both word and gospel.
Weaker vessel does not imply moral or spiritual inferiority but physical or social vulnerability. Honor compensates for vulnerability.
Schreiner emphasizes that the household codes assume existing social structures while transforming them from within. Mutual respect and spiritual equality coexist with ordered roles.
Jobes notes that many women in Asia Minor churches were married to unbelievers - conversion often divided households. Peter addresses their real situation pastorally.

heb.paragraph: Do not repay evil for evil or reviling for reviling, but on the contrary, bless - the distinctive Christian response transforms hostility with grace.
Always be prepared to make a defense to anyone who asks you for a reason for the hope that is in you - the famous text for Christian apologetics.
Keep your conscience clear - so that when you are slandered, those who revile your good behavior may be put to shame.

panels.ctx: Peter summarizes: unity of mind, sympathy, brotherly love, tender heart, humble mind. Do not repay evil with evil but with blessing - for to this you were called. He quotes Psalm 34:12-16: the Lord watches over the righteous but opposes evildoers. The application: who will harm you if you are zealous for good? Yet if you should suffer for righteousness, you are blessed. Do not fear; sanctify Christ as Lord. Always be ready to give a defense for your hope - with gentleness and respect, keeping a good conscience. Better to suffer for doing good than for doing evil.

cross.note: Whoever desires to love life and see good days, let him keep his tongue from evil.
Repay no one evil for evil, but give thought to do what is honorable in the sight of all.
Blessed are those who are persecuted for righteousness sake.
Do not fear what they fear; the LORD of hosts, him you shall honor as holy.

notes.note: Unity, sympathy, love, tender heart, humble mind. Do not repay evil for evil but bless. The Lord watches over the righteous.
Who will harm you if you are zealous for good? Even if you suffer for righteousness, you are blessed. Do not fear their threats.
Sanctify Christ as Lord. Always be prepared to give a defense for your hope - with gentleness and respect. Keep a good conscience.
Calvin notes that blessing enemies is the opposite of natural instinct. Grace transforms retaliation into intercession.
The defense is not aggressive but responsive - given when asked. Gentleness and respect characterize Christian apologetics.
The term apologia (defense) is a legal term - a formal reply to accusation. Christians must have reasons for their hope, not merely feelings.
Good conscience (syneidesis agathe) is a recurring theme in Peter. Clear conscience enables bold witness.
Schreiner emphasizes that v. 15 is programmatic for Christian witness: hope prompts questions; answers require preparation; manner must match message.
Jobes notes that the readers were being slandered as social deviants. Peter strategy is not defensive withdrawal but proactive goodness that shames accusers.

heb.paragraph: Christ suffered once for sins - the sacrifice is unrepeatable. Once accomplished, it need not be repeated. The finality contrasts with OT sacrifices.
Made alive in the spirit - the realm of resurrection life. Christ died in the flesh but was made alive in the spirit.
Baptism corresponds to this - not removal of dirt but appeal to God for a good conscience through the resurrection of Jesus Christ.

panels.ctx: This is one of the most debated passages in the NT. Christ suffered once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring us to God. He was put to death in the flesh but made alive in the spirit - in which he went and proclaimed to the spirits in prison from the days of Noah. Baptism now corresponds to this as an antitype - not physical washing but an appeal to God for a good conscience through resurrection. Christ has gone into heaven and is at God right hand, with angels and authorities subject to him.

cross.note: The death he died he died to sin, once for all.
Christ, having been offered once to bear the sins of many.
The wickedness of humanity in Noah day; the flood narrative.
We were buried with him by baptism into death, so that we might walk in newness of life.

notes.note: Christ suffered once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring us to God. Put to death in flesh, made alive in spirit.
He went and proclaimed to spirits in prison who formerly did not obey during Noah patience while the ark was being prepared.
Baptism corresponds to this - not removal of dirt but appeal to God for a good conscience. Christ is at God right hand with all authority.
Calvin interprets the spirits in prison as the souls of Noah contemporaries to whom Christ preached through Noah. This avoids descent to hell speculations.
Baptism is not magical but sacramental - an appeal to God for a clean conscience. The power is in resurrection, not water.
The spirits in prison is notoriously difficult. Options include: fallen angels (1 Enoch tradition), disobedient humans of Noah day, or souls in Hades.
Antitypos (antitype) indicates that baptism corresponds to Noah flood. Both involve passage through water to salvation.
Schreiner takes the spirits as fallen angels of Genesis 6 and 1 Enoch. Christ proclamation declares his victory over hostile powers.
Jobes emphasizes the pastoral point: Christ victory over hostile powers assures suffering believers that no spiritual force can ultimately harm them.

rows.label: vv. 8-17
rows.text: Blessing instead of reviling; defense of hope

rows.label: vv. 18-22
rows.text: Christ suffered once; baptism and victory

lit.note: Chapter 3 completes the household code and develops the theology of suffering. The enigmatic vv. 18-22 affirm Christ victory over all powers.

themes.note: Sovereignty appears in Christ victory over all powers. Mission is strong in the apologetic call to defend hope. Holiness pervades the ethical exhortations.
